coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of aluminum pyrophosphate for efficient sorption of U(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, De
Xu, Yanbin
Yang, Lixia
Wang, Feng
Asiri, Abdullah M.
Alamry, Khalid A.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, aluminum pyrophosphate (Al4(P2O7)3) was synthesized successfully and applied to adsorb radioactive uranyl ions from aqueous solutions. The material displayed an excellent sorption capacity of removing U(VI) (12.83mg·g−1), achieved a relatively high adsorption percentage (71%) within an hour. In order to reveal its adsorption mechanism, the materials before and after adsorption of U(VI) were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The factors affecting the adsorption performance, such as shaking time, pH value, ionic strength, temperature and so on, were investigated. Based on the above research, the results demonstrated that the chemical adsorption plays a leading role in the process of adsorption, the adsorption process is more suitable to monolayer adsorption, and the adsorption thermodynamics results suggested the adsorption process was spontaneous (ΔG°<0) and exothermic (ΔH°>0). Moreover, the ionic strength and pH have important influence on the sorption of U(VI). Those researches may be valuable for the application of aluminum pyrophosphate in the area of radioactive water pollution governance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Al4(P2O7)3
Synthesis
Sorption capacity
U(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of pH and ionic strength

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Science and Technology Development Plan (NO. J17KA006 ) from Shandong Provincial Education Department .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of synthesized Al4(P2O7)3: (a) before and (b) after adsorption.
The scanning electron microscopy of Al4(P2O7)3: (c) before and (d) after adsorption.
FT-IR of synthesized Al4(P2O7)3: (a) before and (b) after adsorption.

(a) sorption percentage of U(VI) by Al4(P2O7)3, and there different kinetic models (b) pseudo-first-order, (c) pseudo-second-order, (d) Weber–Morris model T=293K, m/V=2.0g·L−1 (Al4(P2O7)3), C0 =30.0mg·L−1 (U(VI)), pH=5.5±0.1.

Effect of initial pH adsorption property of Al4(P2O7)3 (a) 0.001, 0.01 and 0.1mol/L NaClO4 solutions, Co =30mg·L−1, m/V=2.0g·L−1, and T=293K. The relative distribution of U(VI) species as a function of pH (b) in aqueous solutions. Zeta-potential of Al4(P2O7)3 (c) 0.001mol/L NaClO4 solutions, m/V=2.0g·L−1.

Sorption isotherms of Al4(P2O7)3 for U(VI) at different temperatures. (The dotted lines are Langmuir model simulation, and the solid lines are the Freundlich model simulation) pH=5.5±0.1, m/V=2.0g·L−1, T=293, 308, and 323K, I =0.001mol/L NaClO4.
(a) Relationship curve between lnKd and Ce, (b) Relationship curve lnKd 0 and 1/T. pH=5.5±0.1, m/V=2.0 g·L−1,I=0.001mol/L NaClO4.
Adsorption process and mechanisms of UO2 2+ onto Al4(P2O7)3.
Kinetic parameters for the adsorption of U(VI) on the Al4(P2O7)3.

q
t
= K int ⋅ t 1/2 + C

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The relative amounts of species of U(VI) as a function of pH.
Parameters for the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models.
Comparison of U(VI) sorption capacities of Al4(P2O7)3 with raw natural sorbents.
Values of thermodynamic parameters for U(VI) sorption onto Al4(P2O7)3.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of aluminum pyrophosphate for efficient sorption of U(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, aluminum pyrophosphate (Al4(P2O7)3) was synthesized successfully and applied to adsorb radioactive uranyl ions from aqueous solutions. The material displayed an excellent sorption capacity of removing U(VI) (12.83mg·g−1), achieved a relatively high adsorption percentage (71%) within an hour. In order to reveal its adsorption mechanism, the materials before and after adsorption of U(VI) were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The factors affecting the adsorption performance, such as shaking time, pH value, ionic strength, temperature and so on, were investigated. Based on the above research, the results demonstrated that the chemical adsorption plays a leading role in the process of adsorption, the adsorption process is more suitable to monolayer adsorption, and the adsorption thermodynamics results suggested the adsorption process was spontaneous (ΔG°<0) and exothermic (ΔH°>0). Moreover, the ionic strength and pH have important influence on the sorption of U(VI). Those researches may be valuable for the application of aluminum pyrophosphate in the area of radioactive water pollution governance.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to alleviate the growing energy crisis, people are anxious to find new energy sources to replace conventional fossil fuels in recent years [1]. The rapidly developing nuclear energy is considered to be an effective measure to solve the energy crisis [1,2]. But the following problem is that some hypertoxic and radioactive nuclides (such as U, Np, Pu and Am) have been unavoidably interspersed into natural environment in the processes of the mining, extraction, handling and storage. Currently, uranium is the main nuclear fuel and used by nuclear power. Because of its high chemical activity, toxicity and water-solubility, it would bring serious radioactive harms to human health and other organisms when it was inhaled from contaminated groundwater, soil or air [2,3]. In order to solve this problem and avoid further pollution, some scientific methods have been studied and applied to deal with the difficulty of removing toxic and radioactive ions from wastewater. For example, uranium co-precipitation with iron oxide minerals [4], toxic ion removal from wastewater by ion exchange resins [5], treatment of the wastewater containing low-level 241Am using flocculation-microfiltration process [6], biosorption behaviors of uranium (VI) from aqueous solution by sunflower straw [7] and so on. In these methods, adsorption has been achieved widespread applications in the after treatment of radioactive wastes, on account of those advantages, such as easy operation, low-cost, convenient of desorption and high recycling reuse [2–7].

Some phosphate materials could be used for the engineered barriers to isolate waste in the radioactive waste geological disposal on account of the radiation resistance, low-solubility and selective adsorption of radioactive ions [8–10]. Up to now, several research about phosphate solid adsorption of U(VI) were reported. Dort et al. [8] found that the sorption mechanisms of U(VI) onto ZrP2O7, Zr2O(PO4)2 and Th4(PO4)4P2O7) can be explained by using a surface complexation model. Qian et al. [9] studied the adsorption thermodynamics of the removing of U(VI) by ZrP2O7 and LaPO4. Dort et al. [11] also have investigated the sorption of U(VI) onto LaPO4 and found that chemical adsorption was dominant. In recent years, it has become a hotspot research to increase adsorption capacity by modified materials. Some phosphate-functionalized materials show higher adsorption capacity of U(VI) than before, such as phosphate-functionalized graphene oxide (PGO) [2],phosphate-coated hydroxyapatite [12]. Those results indicated that strong complexation between phosphorus-related functional groups and U(VI) [2,12]. In the other hand, the above mentioned compounds of Zr, La and Th are rare, toxic, and easily bring secondary pollution. As everyone knows, modified Hummers method were often used to synthesis graphene oxide and modified graphene oxide, but the preparation process is complex, time-consuming, harsh and usually using strong oxidants such as KMnO4, H2SO4 [2,13,14]. But beyond that, some carbonaceous materials have also been proposed to have potential toxic effects on cells, microorganisms, animals, and environment [15,16]. Therefore, finding a kind of phosphate sorbents with the abundant raw material, low-cost, low toxicity and high adsorption capacity has become more significant. Aluminum pyrophosphate has above advantages of low raw material cost, non-toxic, insolubility, easy preparation and easy adsorption operation, it may be a good choice to replace the natural clay minerals.

In this study, simple solid-state reaction method was used to prepared aluminum pyrophosphate. SEM, FT-IR and XRD were applied to characterize aluminum pyrophosphate samples before and after adsorption. Influence factors of removal U(VI), such as pH value, ionic strength and temperature were investigated. The adsorption principle of U(VI) onto prepared aluminum pyrophosphate material also was discussed in detail. Experimental data shows that Al4(P2O7)3 has a good adsorption capacity of removing U(VI) and may offer some useful proposals of removing radioactive ions from wastewater.
All the chemical reagents were derived from the Mingguang mining industry (Anhui China), were all analytical grade, and were used without further purification.

Preparation of precursor: (NH4)2HPO4 (2.5164g) and ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 16.6mL) were put into an agate mortar and evenly milled to suspension fluid. Then Al(NO3)3·9H2O (4.7655g) was put into the above suspension fluid and grinded it to be white powder. The whole grinding operation was under the infrared light.

Solid-state reaction: the white powder was put into muffle with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 (degrees Celsius per minute), and was kept 400°C for 10h. After that, the sample was cooled naturally to room temperature. After carefully grinding, the Al4(P2O7)3 was successful prepared and preserved for sorption experiment. The experiment process was shown in Fig. 1 .

The as-prepared Al4(P2O7)3 samples were measured and determined by SEM, FT-IR and XRD. The morphology of samples was observed by JSM–5610LV. The XRD patterns were identified by power XRD using a D/max 2500 PC (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu kα radiation (λ=0.15406nm). Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded with KBr pellets by Nicolet 8700 spectrometer at room temperature.

The prepared Al4(P2O7)3 was mixed into a certain concentration of suspended solution (2.0g/L) and then the prepared NaClO4 was also added to the above suspended solution, at last the prepared U(VI) solution was added. The aim of the experimental operation was to achieve the expected ion strength and different components concentration. The pH was adjusted by adding negligible volumes of the prepared HClO4 or NaOH. The test tubes were shaken for 24h to obtain the sorption equilibrium, the solid phase was centrifugalized (9800rpm, 20min) at normal temperature. Arsenazo-III was indicator, the concentration of U(VI) was tested at the wavelength of 669nm. As shown in the following Eqs. (1) and (2), the sorption results could be accurate calculated in terms of sorption percentage (%) and distribution coefficient (Kd):
(1)
S orption % =
C 0
−
C e
C 0
× 100 %
(2)
K d
=
C 0
−
C e
C e
×
V m
where C0 is the initial concentration of U(VI) solutions (mg·L−1), and Ce is the equilibrium concentration of U(VI) solutions (mg·L−1), m is the adsorbent mass (g), and V is the suspension volume (mL) [17,18]. All the experimental data were the averages of triplicate determinations, and the relative errors were <5%.

The XRD and SEM results of Al4(P2O7)3 samples before and after adsorption are shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a,the observed diffraction peaks (2θ=15.430°,16.945°, 18.364°, 20.211°, 21.458°, 22.833°, 26.569°, 28.811°, 30.469°,34.444°, 35.480°, 37.160°) were attributed to the Al4(P2O7)3. Similar diffraction peaks indicated the crystalline Al4(P2O7)3 structure were maintained during the sorption process. Obviously, the diffraction peaks (2θ=20.211°, 21.458°, 30.469°, 35.480°) become more higher, which illustrated the formations of UO3 (JCPDS card NO. 42-0003) and UO2(PO3)2 (JCPDS card NO. 34-1258) were existed after adsorption.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of Al4(P2O7)3 samples were shown in Fig. 2c. It can be seen that the synthesized Al4(P2O7)3 are shown as a large aggregate of ball shape with irregular and rough surface. From Fig. 2d, samples after adsorption, the surface of aggregate become relatively smooth and surface voids become much less. Therefore, the irregular and rough of surfaces may be beneficial for the removing of U(VI) from aqueous solutions.

The samples FT-IR results are shown in Fig. 3 . Refer to earlier research works [19,20], peaks between 470 and 1400cm−1 are considered as the vibration modes of P2O7 4−, those peaks are the PO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration in the range of 1250–975cm−1 and 975–900cm−1, the POP bride vibration around 750cm−1, respectively. In Fig. 3, the remarkable feature of pyrophosphate spectra (POP asymmetric bridge vibrations) is observed at 768cm−1. The peaks at 1137 and 957cm−1 can be seen as the PO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1207 and 1384cm−1 are attributed to the PO stretching vibration. Comparing the samples before and after adsorbed, we also can find that the PO and P-O-P band changed a little.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the kinetic adsorption of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 with different contact times were carried out and matched with pseudo-second-order, pseudo-first-order and Weber–Morris model. In Fig. 4a, the adsorption percentage increased rapidly to 71% within 1h, then increased slowly with the extension of contact time. The maximum adsorption percentage was 77% after 6h. It is expected that the adsorption percentage will be higher than 77% with the longer contact time. The adsorption capacity has the same trend, as shown in Fig. 4b, the maximum adsorption capacity was 6.56mg·g−1 with a contact time of 6h. In this study, three kinds of models (pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and Weber–Morris models) were applied to analyze the kinetic adsorption dates and the results are shown in Table 1 . The k1 and k2 are the pseudo-first-order and the pseudo-second-order rate constant, respectively; qe and qt are the removal capacities at equilibrium time and at time t; Kist is the diffusion rate constant and C is a constant [21–23]. The corresponding units were listed in the Table 1.

Experimental dates were simulated by the pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and Weber–Morris models in Fig. 4b, c and d, respectively. From Table 1, it is clear that the correlation coefficient of pseudo-second-order model (R2 =0.9997) is higher than the pseudo-first-order (R2 =0.8552) and Weber–Morris model (R2 =0.8552). Considering the simulation results of maximum adsorption capacity, the maximum adsorption capacity of the pseudo-second-order model (6.5634mg·g−1) is closer to the kinetic experiment date (6.56mg·g−1), also indicated that the kinetic adsorption dates are better suit for the pseudo-second-order model. The pseudo-second-order model assumes that the adsorption process is a chemical reaction process, indicated that the removing of U(VI) by Al4(P2O7)3 was a chemical reaction process. The adsorbent of Al4(P2O7)3 and adsorbate of U(VI) both have an effect on this adsorption process [23–27].

In order to study the influencing factors in the control step of the adsorption process, the kinetic adsorption dates were analyzed by the Weber–Morris model (intraparticle diffusion model) [28]. From Fig. 4d, we can find that the changes of qt with t1/2 is a multilinear relation, and no stage passes through the origin, suggesting that the intraparticle diffusion is not the only rate controlling step and there are over one control process in the adsorption process (ion exchange or surface complexation) [21]. In this study, the first linear lines represents the U(VI) diffusing into the surface of Al4(P2O7)3 from the solution. The decrease of the active sites and the low concentration of U(VI) diffusing inner the block of Al4(P2O7)3 are fitted for the second linear lines. The control steps are always limited to the minimum diffusion rate constant (Kint) [28]. Comparing Kint1 (2.1944) and Kint2 (0.3345), we can find that the chemical adsorption is the control step of this sorption process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH and ionic strength
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the effect of pH on the removing of U(VI) by Al4(P2O7)3 in different concentration of NaClO4 solution system. Obviously, the pH and ionic strength are both important parameters to the adsorption capacity of the materials. As shown in Fig. 5a, the adsorption percentage of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 increases at pH2.0–6.0, decreases with the increasing pH from 6.0 to 10.0. The adsorption tendency can be explained by the presence form of U(VI) and the surface properties of Al4(P2O7)3 at different pH values. Fig. 5b shows the relative distribution of U(VI) species with the different pH. The chemical equilibrium programs and related parameters (log K) are listed in Table 2 . Fig. 5c displays the Zeta-potential of Al4(P2O7)3 at different pH. From the Fig. 5c, we can know that the Zeta potential values of Al4(P2O7)3 changes to negative at pH>pHzpc (about 2.7) and the negative changes become more distinct with the pH increasing. According to the result, the proton desorption occurs at pH about 2.7. From the Fig. 5b, the uranium mainly exists in the form of positively charged species (UO2 2+, UO2(OH)+) in the pH range 2.0–6.0. Due to the proton desorption from solid surface and electrostatic attraction [29], the adsorption of uranium onto Al4(P2O7)3 increases with pH from 2.0 to 6.0. In the pH range of 6.0–7.0, existence forms of UO2(OH)2 and UO2CO3 increased and UO2(OH)+ reduced. The less uranium in the form of positive charge explains the reducing adsorption capacity of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 with increasing pH from 6.0 to 7.0. In the pH range of 7.0–10.0, the uranium exists in the forms of negatively charged species UO2(CO3)2 2− and UO2(CO3)3 4− [2], the adsorption capacity of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 is falling fast due to electrostatic repulsion [18].

The adsorption percentage decrease in different extent with the increasing ionic strength, the increasing presence of cations in aqueous solution affects the activity coefficients of metal ions, then limits the sorption of U(VI) on Al4(P2O7)3 [21]. Therefore, the dominant sorption mechanism is mainly attributed to the outer-sphere surface complexation or ion exchange rather than inner-sphere surface complexation [18,22].

Adsorption isotherm is fundamental in understanding the distribution of the adsorbate on adsorbent surfaces [11]. The sorption isotherms of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 are shown in Fig. 6 . With the increasing of initial U(VI) concentrations, the adsorption capacity of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 gradually increases and then keep a relatively constant adsorption capacity. It is clear that the adsorption capacity of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 increases with the temperature increasing (5.33mg·g−1 at 293K, 6.07mg·g−1 at 308K, 6.80mg·g−1 at 323K). The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were adopted to simulate the experimental data.

The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models are expressed as [30–33]:
(3)
C e
q e
=
1
q max
b
+
C e
q max
(4)
q e
=
K F
C e n
where Ce and qe have been explained in the above; qmax is the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 at complete monolayer coverage (mg·g−1); b (L·g−1) is the constant and relates to the sorption heat; KF (mol1−n·Ln·g−1) and n are the Freundlich constants, relates to the adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity of the sorbent, respectively [34,35].

The relative parameters of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models are displayed in Table 3 . It is obvious that the correlation coefficient of Langmuir model is always better than that of Freundlich model, regardless of temperature. The Langmuir model is used for modeling the mosolayer coverage of sorption surface and assumes that sorption occurs on a surface containing a finite number of identical sites, suggesting that U(VI) is almost complete monolayer coverage of Al4(P2O7)3, the limited adsorption capacity also proved this result [18,36]. The qmax value of Al4(P2O7)3 towards U(VI) calculated from Langmuir model suggests that increasing temperature was benefited to this adsorption process. The increasing temperature may be benefited for increasing the number of sorption sites and the activity coefficient of U(VI) in solution [23,37,38,39]. Table 4 shows the qmax values of U(VI) sorption on some raw natural sorbents. Al4(P2O7)3 has a higher sorption capacity 12.83mg·g−1 at 293K, compared with attapulgite (2.00mg·g−1) [35], hematite (3.36mg·g−1) [40], bentonite (4.61mg·g−1) [41], diatomite (7.25mg·g−1) [42] and kaolin (4.52mg·g−1) [43]. Therefore, Al4(P2O7)3 can be used as a kind of shielding material with good application prospect in the field of radioactive waste treatment.

The thermodynamic parameters, such as ΔH°, ΔS° and ΔG°, can be figure out by using the sorption isotherms under the three different temperature, and the positive and negative values of these parameters can further reveal the adsorption behavior of U(VI) on Al4(P2O7)3. ΔH° and ΔS° can be obtained from the following formula:
(5)
ln
K d °
=
Δ
S °
R
−
Δ
H °
RT
where distribution coefficient (Kd) can be gotten from the formula 2. Based on the fitting lines (Fig. 7 ), lnK d ° will be calculated when Ce takes to zero [2]. T represents absolute temperature and R (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1). The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°) is calculated by the van't Hoff equation:
(6)
Δ
H °
= Δ
G °
+ T ⋅ Δ
S °

Table 5 shows the results of the thermodynamic parameters. The positive enthalpy change (ΔH°=2.2526KJ·mol−1) indicates that the adsorption process of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 is a endothermic process. The negative free energy changes (ΔG°=−4.1372KJ·mol−1 at 293K) become more negative with increasing of temperature (−4.4643KJ·mol−1 at 308K and −4.7914KJ·mol−1 at 323K), which means that the adsorption process is a spontaneous process and easier with increasing of temperature [44]. The positive entropy change (ΔS°=21.8082J·mol−1·K−1) indicates the adsorption process will increase randomness, which also reveals U(VI) adsorption on Al4(P2O7)3 is a spontaneous process. Those results are benefit to improve the adsorption of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 in the application process.

Comparing the morphology of samples before and after adsorption from Fig. 2c and d, it was obvious that the surface of Al4(P2O7)3 became relatively smooth and the surface voids became much less after adsorption. In Fig. 2a and b, several diffraction peaks after adsorption became more higher, and those peaks could be corresponded to UO3 (JCPDS card NO. 42-0003) and UO2(PO3)2 (JCPDS card NO. 34-1258). Therefore, the changes of surface could be ascribed to the adsorption of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 particles. In fact, the adsorbed U(VI) would further hydrolyze and dehydrate during the dry process [34]. Comparing the FT-IR spectra of Al4(P2O7)3 before and after U(VI) sorption (Fig. 3), some relatively obvious displacements among the PO and PO (from 1137.44cm−1 to 1134.60cm−1, from 957.65cm−1 to 956.91cm−1 and from 1207.19 to 1206.24cm−1, respectively) indicated that the adsorption of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 was a chemical adsorption process. Similar trend was reported in other phosphate system, such as phosphate-functionalized graphene oxide [2] and Fe4(P2O7)3 [23]. Based on those results, the sorption process and major mechanism were described and shown in the Fig. 8 . Uranium ions (UO2 2+) are quickly adsorbed on the surface of Al4(P2O7)3, and then UO2 2+ and POH2 + have an ion exchange process in the surface of Al4(P2O7)3 samples [23], the surface effect of abundance cavities and gaps in Al4(P2O7)3 particles make the uranium ions transferring into the interior of the sample slowly.

In summary, Al4(P2O7)3 have been successfully synthesized by an easy solid-state reaction, which exhibits a higher sorption capacity 12.83mg·g−1 at 293K than some raw natural sorbents. XRD and SEM results demonstrate that U(VI) is adsorbed in the surface of Al4(P2O7)3. The FT-IR spectra of phosphate group slightly shift after adsorption indicates a chemical adsorption process. This result is also in keeping with the result of kinetic adsorption dates. The sorption of U(VI) onto Al4(P2O7)3 is strongly dependent on pH and ionic strength, and can be well described by the Langmuir model. According to the study of adsorption thermodynamics, the sorption process is an endothermic and spontaneous process. All evidence shows that Al4(P2O7)3 can be considered as a high-efficiency adsorbent to remove or store radioactive wastes due to the advantages of low raw material cost, non-toxic, insolubility, easy adsorption operation and relatively higher adsorption capacity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Science and Technology Development Plan (NO. J17KA006) from Shandong Provincial Education Department.
